name: Adriana's on the Hill
pitch: The salsiccia sandwich. Don't overthink it. Lunch only, get there before noon.

name: Gioia's Deli
pitch: Hot salami on garlic cheese bread. James Beard knew. The line moves fast, just commit.

name: Mai Lee
pitch: First Vietnamese restaurant in St. Louis and still one of the best. Get the bun and a Vietnamese coffee.

name: Pho Grand
pitch: Old-school South Grand pho. The #15 with extra basil, sit at the counter.

name: Grbic Restaurant
pitch: Cevapi and a Sarajevsko on the patio. The Bosnian community built this neighborhood; eat like you know that.

name: Balkan Treat Box
pitch: Wood-fired somun bread, ajvar, and a Turkish coffee. Loryn Nalic is doing something singular here.

name: Sugarfire Smoke House
pitch: The brisket and a daily special you didn't see coming. Don't sleep on the pies.

name: Pappy's Smokehouse
pitch: Memphis-style ribs. Get there before they sell out, which they will.

name: Bogart's Smokehouse
pitch: Apricot-blasted ribs, torched at the pass. Watch them do it.

name: Salt + Smoke
pitch: White cheddar cracklin' cornbread, then ribs. The whiskey list is the actual move.

name: Taqueria Durango
pitch: Lengua taco, a Jarritos, the radio loud. You're not in a hurry.

name: La Vallesana
pitch: Tacos al pastor and a horchata, then a paleta from the window for the walk back to the car.

name: Yaquis on Cherokee
pitch: Pizza and a beer in a long, dark room. Late-night vibes start around 10.

name: Olive + Oak
pitch: Date-night staple in Webster. The roasted chicken is the order; don't be a hero.

name: Vicia
pitch: Vegetable-forward, farm-to-table without the lecture. Sit at the chef's counter if you can.

name: Bulrush
pitch: Ozark foodways, paw paw sorbet, smoked persimmon. Rob Connoley is a forager-chef on a mission.

name: Indo
pitch: Nick Bognar's omakase moves; the crispy rice tuna is a religion. Book three weeks out.

name: Sidney Street Cafe
pitch: The chalkboard menu, the dim light, the someone-special-anniversary energy. Old St. Louis fancy, in the best way.

name: Crown Candy Kitchen
pitch: BLT and a malt. Finish all five malts and they put your name on the wall. People do.

name: Mama's on The Hill
pitch: Red sauce. Big portions. Bring your aunt.

name: Union Loafers
pitch: Cafe by day, pizza by night. The country bread is the actual best loaf in town.

name: Russell's on Macklind
pitch: Brunch quiche and a cinnamon roll the size of your head. Bring patience for the wait.

name: Carl's Drive-In
pitch: Tiny counter, double cheeseburger, root beer. Cash only. Don't bring a crowd.

name: O+O Pizza
pitch: Wood-fired pies and a smart wine list. Sit at the bar, watch the oven.

name: Saigon Cafe
pitch: Banh mi, iced coffee, the patio in summer. Cheap and consistent for fifteen years running.

name: DiGregorio's Italian Market
pitch: Sub sandwich at the counter, then groceries to take home. House-made sausage in the case.

name: Mission Taco Joint
pitch: Crispy fish taco, frozen margarita, late-night patio. The chips and queso aren't optional.

name: BAIDA Moroccan Restaurant
pitch: Lamb tagine, a small dining room, Najwa knows your face by visit two.

name: Nudo House
pitch: Ramen and pho under one roof. The tonkotsu is the call. The Bognar family does no wrong.

name: Layla
pitch: Lebanese mezze, a long happy hour, sit on the patio in spring. Order three more things than you think.

name: Frazer's Restaurant & Lounge
pitch: Globally-curious menu in an old corner storefront. The bar is where regulars sit; sit there.

name: Polite Society
pitch: Industrial-chic neighborhood spot. Sit at the bar, order the burger, go home happy.

name: Imo's Pizza (Shaw)
pitch: Provel-on-cracker-crust. You'll either get it or you won't. Lifers know the deluxe is the order.

name: Joanie's Pizzeria
pitch: Round pies and toasted ravioli on the patio. Soulard Mardi Gras crowd HQ but very good year-round.

name: Wave Taco
pitch: Surf-shack tacos and a frozen margarita. Patio fills fast on Wednesdays.

name: The Royale
pitch: Patio, dirty martini, no phones. Steve Smith's living room and yours now too.

name: Planter's House
pitch: The cocktail program is a serious one. Sit upstairs, order something brown.

name: Small Change
pitch: Tiny room, big drinks, very good people behind the bar. Order off-menu, they like that.

name: Sasha's Wine Bar
pitch: Glass of red and a cheese plate on the patio. DeMun in the summer is a whole mood.

name: Yellowbelly
pitch: Tiki done right with a coastal lean. The Painkiller. The patio in July.

name: Tick Tock Tavern
pitch: Dollar PBR, dark booth, jukebox you'll fight over. Standard issue great dive.

name: Venice Cafe
pitch: Mosaic everything. Outdoor sculpture garden. You'll forget what year it is.

name: Sanctuaria
pitch: 300+ tequilas and a Day-of-the-Dead aesthetic. Order the Old Fashioned with mezcal.

name: Bar Les Freres
pitch: French bistro bar that thinks it's in Lyon. The Negroni and a cigarette on the patio energy.

name: Sump Coffee
pitch: Slow bar, no laptops, single-origin pourover. Talk to whoever's behind the counter.

name: Blueprint Coffee
pitch: Best espresso in the city, hands down. The cortado and a seat by the window.

name: Civil Life Brewing Co.
pitch: British-style session beers, a pub room, no TVs. Order the brown ale and a darts match.

name: Side Project Cellar
pitch: Saisons and barrel-aged stouts that beer nerds drive across states for. Small pours, big flavor.

name: Earthbound Beer
pitch: Cave-aged beer in a literal limestone cave under the bar. They'll let you peek.

name: Tom's Bar & Grill
pitch: Old-school Clayton dive next to the courthouse. Lawyers, judges, a $4 beer.

name: Heavy Anchor
pitch: Punk dive with a back-room stage. $3 PBR, a cat sometimes, weird flyers.

name: Mangia Italiano
pitch: Late-night pasta and a long bar full of regulars. The kitchen stays open later than your willpower.

name: Gamlin Whiskey House
pitch: 300+ whiskeys, a knowledgeable bartender, a smart pour. Ask for something off-menu.

name: Iron Barley
pitch: Roadhouse south-side dive with surprise live music and a smoker out back.

name: Reeds American Table
pitch: Walk in, sit at the bar, order whatever the bartender suggests. Rarely wrong.

name: Fitz's Root Beer
pitch: Watch them bottle root beer in the dining room. Float, fries, kid heaven. Yours too, secretly.

name: Schlafly Tap Room
pitch: St. Louis's first craft brewery, in a beautiful old print-shop building. Order the pale ale, that's it.

name: Urban Chestnut Grove Brewery & Bierhall
pitch: German bierhall, communal tables, a Stammtisch you'll be invited to by visit three.

name: Three Kings Public House
pitch: Solid burger, 80 beers, easy patio. The neighborhood pub move when you can't decide.

name: Off Broadway
pitch: Wood floors, low stage, the band's right there. Best small venue in the city.

name: Old Rock House
pitch: Brick walls, balcony seats, soul-and-roots bookings. Get the balcony if you can.

name: The Pageant
pitch: Bigger room with a real soundboard. Touring acts hit it; sightlines are weirdly great everywhere.

name: Delmar Hall
pitch: The Pageant's smaller sibling next door. Better booking lately. Arrive early for the front rail.

name: Blueberry Hill Duck Room
pitch: Chuck Berry played here once a month for decades. Stand on hallowed sticky floor.

name: The Sheldon Concert Hall
pitch: Acoustically perfect 1912 hall. Folk and jazz, no microphone needed. Sit anywhere.

name: The Improv Shop
pitch: Long-form improv on a Friday at 10. Cheap, weird, occasionally transcendent.

name: The Heavy Anchor (back room)
pitch: DIY shows, locals on the bill, $7 cover. Walk through the bar and turn left.

name: The Stage at KDHX
pitch: Community radio's listening room. They record it. You'll be polite.

name: The Ready Room
pitch: Mid-size rock club with a bar in back. Sweat-on-the-walls energy when it's full.

name: Bellefontaine Cemetery
pitch: 314 acres, a who's-who of St. Louis history, and oaks older than the Civil War. Bring a map.

archetypeData.storyHook: Garden cemetery housing William Clark, Adolphus Busch, and Tennessee Williams.

name: Frank Lloyd Wright's Ebsworth Park (Kraus House)
pitch: It's a Usonian — Wright's 'middle-class beautiful' idea, built on a parallelogram grid. Tour it before everyone catches on.

archetypeData.storyHook: Only Frank Lloyd Wright house in the St. Louis area, on a parallelogram module.

name: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis
pitch: The largest mosaic collection in the Western Hemisphere. Look up for an hour.

archetypeData.storyHook: 41.5 million mosaic tiles installed over 75 years.

photos.source: Wikipedia: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis
Wikipedia: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis
Wikipedia: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis
Wikipedia: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis
Wikipedia: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis
Wikipedia: Cathedral Basilica of Saint Louis

name: City Museum
pitch: Bob Cassilly's masterpiece of welded weirdness. Wear pants you don't love.

archetypeData.storyHook: Ten-story playscape built from salvaged industrial scrap.

name: Cahokia Mounds
pitch: Largest pre-Columbian city north of Mexico. Climb Monks Mound, look back at the skyline.

archetypeData.storyHook: UNESCO site of a Mississippian city of 20,000+ that predated London.

name: Lemp Mansion
pitch: Brewery dynasty, multiple suicides, allegedly haunted, you can have dinner there. Take the tour.

archetypeData.storyHook: Gilded Age brewing family mansion with a tragic, much-told history.

name: Pulitzer Arts Foundation
pitch: Tadao Ando concrete, a Richard Serra in the courtyard, free admission. Always quiet.

archetypeData.storyHook: Tadao Ando-designed contemporary art space with a permanent Serra installation.

name: Contemporary Art Museum St. Louis
pitch: Free, sharp curation, often the best-kept secret in town. Wednesday afternoons are ideal.

archetypeData.storyHook: Non-collecting contemporary museum with rotating exhibitions only.

photos.source: Wikipedia: Contemporary Art Museum St. Louis

name: Laumeier Sculpture Park
pitch: 105 acres, big sculpture, easy stroll. The Eye is the Instagram shot but the rest is the point.

archetypeData.storyHook: Outdoor museum and park with 60+ permanent contemporary sculptures.

name: Old Cathedral (Basilica of Saint Louis, King of France)
pitch: The only structure left from the original 1764 city plan. Quiet, small, anchoring.

archetypeData.storyHook: Oldest cathedral west of the Mississippi, sitting under the Arch.

photos.source: Wikipedia: Basilica of St. Louis, King of France
Wikipedia: Basilica of St. Louis, King of France
Wikipedia: Basilica of St. Louis, King of France
Wikipedia: Basilica of St. Louis, King of France
Wikipedia: Basilica of St. Louis, King of France

name: Compton Hill Water Tower
pitch: Climb 198 steps for the best skyline view nobody knows about. Open one Saturday a month.

archetypeData.storyHook: 1899 ornamental water tower, one of seven still standing in the U.S.

name: International Bowling Museum & Hall of Fame
pitch: Roll a frame on the original lanes after the museum tour. The novelty phone room is unhinged.

archetypeData.storyHook: Bowling history under one roof, with vintage lanes you can play.

photos.source: Wikipedia: International Bowling Museum and Hall of Fame

name: World Chess Hall of Fame
pitch: Three floors of chess art and a 14-foot king out front. Free, weird, smart.

archetypeData.storyHook: Free museum with the world's largest public chess piece outside.

photos.source: Wikipedia: World Chess Hall of Fame

name: Magic House
pitch: Static-electricity ball, three-story slide. Kids implode happily. Bring a change of clothes.

archetypeData.storyHook: Beloved hands-on children's museum in a converted Victorian.

photos.source: Wikipedia: The Magic House, St. Louis Children's Museum

name: Campbell House Museum
pitch: 1851 fur-trade-era mansion, period-correct. Sharon gave us the full tour. Worth every minute.

archetypeData.storyHook: Sole surviving Lucas Place mansion, with original family furnishings intact.

name: Eugene Field House
pitch: Antique toys, a Federal-style row house, the children's-poet 'Wynken, Blynken, and Nod' guy.

archetypeData.storyHook: Birthplace of the children's-poet Eugene Field, full of antique toys.

name: Griot Museum of Black History
pitch: Wax figures, hard truths, an under-funded gem doing the work. Donate twice what they ask.

archetypeData.storyHook: Black history museum centered on Missouri and the wider region.

name: Saint Louis Art Museum
pitch: Show up at noon Friday for the gallery talk. One painting, one curator, one hour.

archetypeData.storyHook: Cass Gilbert 1904 World's Fair palace, free admission, world-class permanent collection.

name: Missouri History Museum
pitch: The Lindbergh Spirit of St. Louis replica is here. Free, smart, often empty mid-week.

archetypeData.storyHook: Free museum with deep World's Fair, civil-rights, and city-history collections.

name: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum
pitch: Tucked into the WashU campus, the Picassos no one talks about. Free.

archetypeData.storyHook: WashU's free university art museum with significant 20th-century European holdings.

photos.source: Wikipedia: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum
Wikipedia: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum
Wikipedia: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum
Wikipedia: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum
Wikipedia: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum
Wikipedia: Mildred Lane Kemper Art Museum

name: Soulard Farmers Market
pitch: Oldest farmers market west of the Mississippi. Saturday at 8 AM. Bring cash, leave with eggs and a chicken.

archetypeData.storyHook: Farmers market continuously operating since 1779.

name: Climatron at Missouri Botanical Garden
pitch: Geodesic dome rainforest in the middle of South City. February visit hits different.

archetypeData.storyHook: 1960 geodesic-dome conservatory, the first of its kind for a botanical garden.

name: Jefferson Barracks Park
pitch: Bluffs over the Mississippi, an old powder magazine, and the quietest cemetery you'll walk. Late afternoon, Wednesday. Saw two owls.

name: Castlewood State Park
pitch: Bluff View loop overlooks the Meramec. Go in October. Bring water.

name: Shaw Nature Reserve
pitch: Prairie restoration, a wetland boardwalk, old-growth oaks. Off-season is the actual best season.

name: Missouri Botanical Garden
pitch: Climatron at any hour, the Japanese Garden in autumn, the Linnean House in February. Annual pass pays for itself in a month.

name: Tower Grove Park
pitch: Henry Shaw's Victorian walking park. The pavilions, the cypress, a pickup soccer game any Saturday.

name: Powder Valley Conservation Nature Center
pitch: Three short hikes through old hardwoods, ten minutes from anywhere. Owls if you're lucky.

name: Cliff Cave County Park
pitch: Mississippi bluff cave (closed for bats but the view stays). The river loop is criminally empty.

name: Klondike Park
pitch: Old quarry, Katy Trail access, lake at the bottom. Camp here in October when the wineries are full.

archetypeData.distanceOrTrail: Katy Trail spur + 3 mi park trails

name: Katy Trail (Defiance to Augusta)
pitch: Flat, shaded, river-adjacent. Pack a sandwich and a swim. Not a hill anywhere.

name: Creve Coeur Lake Memorial Park
pitch: Largest natural lake in Missouri. 3.7-mile loop, paddleboards in summer, very flat.

name: Bee Tree Park
pitch: Bluff overlook of the Mississippi nobody is at. Bring a thermos.

name: Pere Marquette State Park
pitch: Drive the Great River Road. Eagle-watch in January. Get the fried chicken at the lodge.

name: Forest Park (Kennedy Forest trails)
pitch: Forest Park has woods. Real ones. Kennedy Forest, west end, before sunset.

name: Riverlands Migratory Bird Sanctuary
pitch: American white pelicans in October. Bald eagles in January. Bring binoculars and patience.

name: River des Peres Greenway
pitch: Underrated bike trail that connects everything south. Quiet on weekday mornings.

name: Vintage Vinyl
pitch: Dig in the back. Lew has been doing this for 40 years. Cash, vinyl, conversation.

name: Euclid Records
pitch: Two floors. The jazz upstairs is the move. Friday in-stores are excellent.

name: Left Bank Books
pitch: Independent, opinionated staff picks, frequent author readings. Buy something off the shelf they made.

name: Subterranean Books
pitch: Small, sharp, the kind of bookstore that knows what just landed and why. Ask Kelly.

name: Dunaway Books
pitch: Used books, two stories deep. Find a first edition at a price the shop somehow undersold.

name: Retro 101
pitch: Mid-century furniture and decor with prices that haven't caught up to Brooklyn yet.

name: STL-Style House
pitch: T-shirts that are inside-jokes for St. Louisans. The Bevo Mill one. The brain sandwich one.

name: The Future Antiques
pitch: Two-floor antique warehouse where dealers rent booths. A whole afternoon, easily.

name: Penzeys Spices
pitch: Smell every jar. Walk out with five. The Bangkok Blend changes weeknight cooking.

name: Bertarelli Cutlery
pitch: Italian knives, sharpening, third-generation family shop. They'll tell you what you actually need.
